quotedTweet.text:
So we keep talking about Ukrainian drones attacking Russian refineries. 

Pulling out my Refinery Configuration Schematic, I see 3 big scenarios:

1⃣If the drones struck the CDU (the crosses in blue), there's refinery downtime and the immediate hit is on distillates production and more crude exports.

2⃣If the drones struck the reformer (the small yellow crosses), you are going to get less mogas but more naphtha exports from Russia. 

3⃣If the drones struck the important secondary units like the FCC and HCU (the big red crosses), you are going to get less mogas and diesel respectively, and more fuel oil exports from Russia (if the CDU is still running). 

Also it usually takes a longer time to repair damaged secondary units vs the CDU due to more sophisticated parts.

I don't have visibility of where the strikes happened, but we can see European product cracks for direction.
 
#oott

text: Iran has attacked at least two (and probably tree) oil/LNG tankers in the last 24 hours in the Strait of Hormuz. If history serves of any guide, the US is likely to retaliate soon against Tehran. All SoH traffic has gone, again, dark, with vessels switching off their AIS.

HEDGE FUNDS and other money managers are much more bullish about the outlook for the price of gasoline than diesel or crude oil. U.S. gasoline inventories were severely depleted during the war between the United States and Iran as refiners prioritised production of more jet fuel. Fund managers have boosted their net position in U.S. gasoline futures and options to a 14-week high equivalent to 72 million barrels (78th percentile for all weeks since 2011). The net position is far more bullish than for diesel (52 million barrels, 43rd percentile) or crude (141 million barrels, 1st percentile):

The prosperity ships got hit?

That’s the end of the Oman lane. For those that were tracking these things closely, VLCCs ending with prosperity or B were going through under US escorts. 

The US can either retaliate and resume escort or Iran will now choke off Oman lane.

#Nigeria: Renaissance Africa Energy said it made a “significant” oil discovery in a Nigerian block once held by a unit of Shell Plc, demonstrating the growing capability of local companies in Africa’s biggest producer.

The JK-004 exploration well, located in shallow water in Oil Mining Lease 74, encountered about 1,000 feet of hydrocarbon-bearing column with an initial analysis detecting light oil, the company said in a statement Tuesday.

Arabica #coffee futures surged as much as 19% on Monday, the steepest one-day rise in 26 years, as El Niño-related weather concerns extended a one-month rally that has lifted prices by 45%. Widespread expectations of a bumper crop in top producer Brazil have been tempered by poor weather that has delayed harvesting, prompting some growers to hold back sales in anticipation of higher prices. Meanwhile, #cocoa futures jumped 13% to their highest since January as persistent rains across key West African growing regions added to supply concerns, squeezing funds holding sizeable short positions.

7.7.26

AI & SEMICONDUCTOR SHOCK
- Samsung Earnings Miss Concerns: Samsung Electronics flagged a 19-fold jump in Q2 operating profit, surpassing its combined earnings over the past three years. However, its shares slid as a slight revenue miss—driven by more moderate DRAM price hikes than anticipated—failed to ease deep structural concerns over the durability of the AI infrastructure boom. The KOSPI index fell as much as 5% on the day.
- Memory Backlash & Rotations: The results triggered heavy profit-taking across global memory names, driving an MSCI Asia technology index down 4.9%. Investors rotated heavily into consumer, financial, and communication sectors. A critical market test looms Friday when South Korea's SK Hynix begins trading its $28 billion ADR package on the Nasdaq.
- Mega-Capex Projections: Despite immediate jitters, consensus forecasts from Visible Alpha show combined Q2 capital spending by Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta surging 74% YoY to hit $168 billion, validating near-term hardware allocations.
- Sun Valley M&A Focus: The annual Allen & Co. Sun Valley conference kicks off today in Idaho. The guest list features top AI figures including OpenAI's Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, and Anthropic's Dario Amodei, turning focus toward potential consolidation and strategic partnerships.

SPACEX NASDAQ-100 INCLUSION
- Passive Inflow Day: SpaceX (SPCX) officially joins the Nasdaq-100 index today. Despite its $2.1 trillion market cap, the rocket and AI giant will carry an initial weight of less than 1% due to Nasdaq's free-float adjustments (SpaceX sold less than 5% of its shares in June's primary offering). Indexers hoarding stock at Monday's close drove an aggressive volume spike.
- Underwriter Coverage Unleashed: The industry-mandated quiet period expired today for underwriting syndicates. At least six major institutions initiated coverage with top-tier buy recommendations. Morgan Stanley labeled the firm "AI's final frontier," while Goldman Sachs, BofA, Citi, and UBS issued highly bullish structural targets focused on Starlink data scalability and Starship reusability. 
- Bank Earnings Tailwind: This historic capital market issuance, coupled with robust M&A advisory fees, will power Q2 sales and trading divisions at major Wall Street banks. JPMorgan, Bank of America, Citigroup, Wells Fargo, and Goldman Sachs report on July 14, with Morgan Stanley following on July 15.

GEOPOLITICS, NATO SUMMIT & ENERGY
- Tanker Attacks in Hormuz: Two commercial vessels were struck overnight in the Strait of Hormuz, fracturing the fragile truce. Fired upon by Iranian forces, the Qatari LNG tanker Al Rekayyat suffered an engine room fire, while a Saudi crude oil supertanker reported localized hull damage. 
- Deadlock and Warnings: Iran's Foreign Ministry announced a complete suspension of peace talks unless President Trump halts his threats to resume full military action. WTI crude remains heavily capped below $70/bbl, though a minor squeeze is developing on short stops.
- NATO Arms Deals: Ahead of a highly charged summit with President Trump, NATO leaders in Turkey unveiled tens of billions in arms agreements. European nations announced major surveillance drone orders from Northrop Grumman (NOC) and defensive aircraft buys from Sweden's Saab, while the US is negotiating domestic European missile production lines to secure Ukraine supply. EU Natural Gas surged 4% on the news.

MACROECONOMICS, RATES & FX
- Extreme Dollar Longs: Global speculators have accumulated a massive $40 billion net-long dollar position—the highest in more than a decade—according to CFTC data released July 6. The greenback’s 2% surge in June was heavily driven by newly sworn-in Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's vow to restore price stability. Markets are currently pricing in roughly 1.24 quarter-point rate hikes through year-end.
- FOMC Minutes Loom: Traders look to Wednesday's release of the June FOMC minutes for technical insights into how Warsh is structuring forward guidance and managing consensus among the nine members who plotted near-term rate hikes before the crude crash.
- Yen Short Cap: Hedge funds turned the most net-short on the yen since 2007. Wide interest-rate differentials continue to fuel yen carry trades into higher-yielding dollars, pushing USD/JPY to a 40-year high of 162.82 as Japanese authorities refrain from active intervention.

FINANCIAL SERVICES REGULATION (DEBIT CARDS)
- Bypassing the Durbin Amendment: Large financial institutions are exploring network acquisitions to circumvent Dodd-Frank limits on debit-card transaction fees. Capital One's $50.6B Discover acquisition highlighted that owning the underlying routing infrastructure provides an exemption from federal interchange caps.
- Fiserv Moves: Attention is turning to financial technology providers that connect Wall Street to retail merchant systems. Fiserv (FISV), which owns the STAR and Accel processing networks, jumped 5% this morning after losing 70% of its market value over the past year.

AGRICULTURE, EL NIÑO & WEATHER SHOCKS
- Cocoa and Coffee Spikes: Cocoa futures in New York surged to their highest absolute level since January due to heavy rainfall in the Ivory Coast and Ghana, raising black pod disease risks. 
- Harvest Delays: Concurrently, unseasonal rainfall in Brazil has severely delayed the local coffee harvest, causing growers to hoard inventory. September coffee futures surged 20% over the last week. Forecasters warn a strengthening El Niño cycle is increasing severe weather and flooding across the Florida peninsula.

CALENDAR
- Tue (Today): 2026 Sun Valley Conference begins. Japan 30Y Treasury Auction, Labor Cash Earnings. EU German Industrial Production. BOE Financial Stability Report. US Trade Balance, JOLTS Job Openings, Redbook Retail Sales. Treasury $58B 3-Year Note Auction. API Weekly Petroleum Report.
- Wed: Germany Wholesale Price Index. UK REC Report on Jobs. US MBA Mortgage Applications, Wholesale Inventories. June FOMC Policy Meeting Minutes (2:00 p.m. ET). US Consumer Credit. DOE Crude and Distillate Inventories. $39B 10-Year Note Auction. Trump meets Erdogan/NATO Leaders in Turkey.
- Thu: China June CPI & PPI. Japan Machine Tool Orders. ECB Account of June Meeting (7:30 a.m. ET). US Weekly Jobless Claims, Existing Home Sales. $22B 30-Year Bond Auction. Fed's Williams, Fed's Logan speak. SK Hynix Final IPO Pricing.
- Fri: Japan PPI. German CPI. Canadian Net Change in Employment. Baker Hughes Rig Count. CFTC Commitment of Traders (3:30 p.m. ET). Fed H8 Bank Balance Sheet Update. SK Hynix Nasdaq trading debut.

7 月 7 日 A 股早盘完整观点解读

外围全线上涨 + 三星业绩炸裂

         昨晚美股、中概集体收红，叠加三星电子二季度利润同比暴涨 19 倍、存储周期逻辑再强化，先理清外围利好与隐藏压制风险，再拆解今日大盘节奏、板块分层策略与核心风险。

一、大盘整体节奏：小幅高开震荡，仅情绪修复、非趋势反转

1、开盘驱动：美股三大指数、中概金龙指数全线上涨，费城半导体收涨，叠加三星超预期存储业绩，今日 A 股小幅高开是大概率；
2、三是港股存在大额解禁抛压，北向资金日内分歧加大，高位科技股容易出现高开兑现；三重压制反弹高度的利空，决定反弹空间有限：一是美国财政部流出报告草案，直接警示 AI 赛道存在泡沫风险，类比 2000 年互联网泡沫，压制全球科技股估值预期；二是韩国股市情绪脆弱，三星业绩公布后韩股高开跳水，存储板块冲高回落，拖累全球半导体情绪；
3、操作纪律：今日冲高缩量就是减仓窗口，不开新仓、不追加仓位。当前行情只是外围利好带来的脉冲式情绪修复，不存在单边反转基础；
4、真正趋势拐点需等待 7 月中旬关键数据与会议：7 月 17/21 日国内 GDP 经济数据落地，叠加重磅政策会议定调，在此之前市场整体维持箱体震荡格局。

二、板块强弱分层：执行杠铃配置策略，进攻短线逢高兑现、防御做底仓对冲

（一）进攻短线主线（仅博弈日内修复，不追高，冲高分批减仓）

存储芯片（今日核心主线，催化最密集）
         覆盖标的：江波龙、兆易创新、佰维存储、深科技，同时延伸 HBM 先进封装长电科技、盛合晶微、配套材料华海诚科。
         多重利好共振：三星二季度利润暴涨核心驱动力是 HBM、DRAM 涨价；SK 海力士启动 280 亿美元美股 ADR 上市；隔夜美股存储板块全线走高；国内 SSD 终端产品价格翻倍上涨，行业供需缺口至少延续至 2027 年。

半导体设备、算力硬件
         博通、AMD 隔夜大涨带动板块情绪，但中长期存在利空约束：英伟达 Rubin 全液冷架构交付延期，限制算力硬件中长期上行空间，仅适合短线日内博弈，不做中线持仓。标的：中微公司、北方华创、紫光股份、中际旭创。

新能源车、传媒游戏
         特斯拉、蔚来、B 站中概股昨夜大幅反弹，整车、游戏板块跟随情绪脉冲，属于纯事件驱动反弹，无新增产业催化，持续性偏弱。

人形机器人
         高盛发布行业测算，2035 年全球人形机器人出货量达 140 万台，十年增长 70 倍，叠加宇树科技 IPO 催化，仅题材脉冲行情，高位标的谨慎追涨。标的：三花智控、先锋电子、晋拓股份。

（二）防御底仓赛道（杠铃配置另一端，平滑科技板块高波动）

创新药、中药
隔夜美股减肥药赛道集体大跌，资金高低切换逻辑明确，震荡市中药、CXO 能稳定账户回撤，作为中长期均衡底仓持有。标的：药明康德、首药控股、康恩贝。

必选消费、养殖、高股息
传统防御板块，对冲科技高位回调风险，适合稳健投资者配置，包含生猪养殖、食品饮料、电力煤炭高股息蓝筹。

航空、化工化纤
国际原油大幅降价，上游原材料成本持续改善，板块业绩确定性稳定，具备波段机会。

三、本周关键风险提示（周三重点盯防）

本周周三将公布美联储议息会议纪要，若纪要措辞偏鹰、释放收紧流动性信号，全球高估值 AI 成长赛道会再度承压，高位存储、光模块、机器人标的回调风险会显著放大。

四、整体操作总结

仓位控制：维持现有仓位，今日不新开仓、不加仓，冲高缩量主动减仓高位科技；
交易思路：进攻板块只做日内低吸博弈反弹，绝不追高开；防御板块底仓不动，平衡账户波动；
中期等待：所有重仓加仓动作，统一延后至 7 月中旬 GDP 数据、重磅会议落地后再判断。

Mid-Day Markets - July 7, 2026

This Morning's Top Story

The macro landscape is establishing an aggressive, multi-asset short-covering rally today, July 7, 2026, as traders rush back from the extended holiday weekend to aggressively re-price domestic crop fundamentals. Risk-management desks have completely reversed last week's pre-holiday defensive stance, shifting away from macro liquidity metrics and returning their focus entirely to regional environmental stress lines appearing across the central United States.

The primary catalyst on today's tape is an unseasonal, intense heat dome developing rapidly across the western Corn Belt. Automated algorithmic models and commodity trading advisors (CTAs) are treating this sudden high-pressure atmospheric block as a major threat to critical mid-summer crop pollination windows. This sudden return of a weather-driven inflation premium has triggered sharp upward moves across multiple agricultural complexes, successfully breaking a multi-week technical descent and forcing institutional short-positioned accounts to aggressively cover their exposures.

Market News (Performance Review & Rebalancing)

Post-holiday capital rebalancing has brought explosive volume to the floor, driving sharp intraday splits between rising agricultural benchmarks and structurally lower energy baselines.

Row Crops Ignite on Heat Risks: The crop complex has completely shaken off last week's holiday long liquidation. CBOT Corn futures are leading the agricultural charge higher, jumping nearly 16 cents today to sit at $4.75 per bushel for front-month delivery. The rally is fueled by a severe deterioration in western Corn Belt soil moisture levels over the holiday stretch, directly forcing short sellers out of their positions. Soybeans are tracking a similarly powerful technical upswing, rocketing over 50 cents higher to print at $11.82 per bushel as automated trend-following networks rush back into the long side of the ledger.

Livestock Stabilizes Post-Holiday: The meat pits are navigating a quiet, consolidative trading path today as paper charts work to find a firm baseline past the July 4th peak. August Live Cattle are holding steady near $239.10, while August Feeder Cattle are demonstrating narrow horizontal trade around $360.50 per hundredweight ($360.50/cwt). Independent operations are carefully digesting a slow post-holiday processing restart, with daily slaughter rates opening the week at a tightly managed 103,000 head.

Wholesale Carcass Values Soften Slightly: Wholesale demand benchmarks are showing localized adjustments following the holiday clearance. Choice boxed beef cutout values eased a fraction by $0.59 to print at $386.48/cwt, while Select boxes pulled back to $365.87/cwt. Despite the minor easing in beef cuts, the underlying physical cash market remains incredibly resilient, with country trade clearing steady at a weighted average of $255.12 across major feeding regions.

Crude Pinned Down by OPEC+ Influx: WTI crude futures are failing to catch any macro tailwinds from the domestic crop surge, stuck near multi-month lows at $68.84 per barrel. The market continues to absorb an expansion of regional production quotas across West Africa and Saudi Arabia. Concurrently, a steady recovery of commercial tanker tracking numbers through the fully reopened Strait of Hormuz has entirely cleared out any lingering transit risk premiums.

Corn (/ZC): Support 460 - Resistance 485 - Status: Bullish Breakout; July contract rocketing 15.75 cents to $4.75 as an intense western Corn Belt heat dome completely wipes out short-selling momentum.

Soybeans (/ZS): Support 1140 - Resistance 1200 - Status: Strongly Bullish; July bean futures surging 50.5 cents to trade at $11.82, tracking severe early-summer environmental stress indicators.

Wheat (/ZW): Support 615 - Resistance 650 - Status: Firming; July KC hard red winter wheat jumping 11.5 cents to settle near $6.38, catching a powerful short-covering lift from row-crop momentum despite ongoing Southern Plains harvest progress.

Live Cattle (/LE): Support 236.00 - Resistance 242.00 - Status: Range-Bound; August contract resting flat at $239.10 as paper futures balance steady $255 country cash trade against minor wholesale cutout adjustments.

Lean Hogs (/HE): Support 88.00 - Resistance 93.00 - Status: Corrective; Carcass equivalents backing down slightly to trace an index baseline base near $90.25 per hundredweight.

Crude Oil (/CL): Support 66.50 - Resistance 71.00 - Status: Heavy Trend; WTI crude pinned down near $68.84 as expanding OPEC+ shipping volumes cap any geopolitical energy recovery.

Gold (/GC): Support 4310 - Resistance 4380 - Status: Weak; Finding minimal traction as roaring agricultural assets siphon speculative money away from passive safe havens.

Chart of the Day: December Corn (/ZCZ26) Technical Reversal
Action: Defensive Short Coverage / Enforce Open Trailing Buy Stops.

Why: December Corn has completely transformed its chart structure today, mounting a violent technical breakout past key overhead short-term moving averages. The sudden, unseasonal expansion of a high-pressure heat dome over core production states like Nebraska has completely invalidated early-summer bearish models that assumed an unblemished growing season.

Tactical Note: The contract is experiencing heavy buy-side volume as automated CTA trend models trigger forced short-covering orders. If the western Corn Belt heat dome secures a firm technical footing through the mid-week sessions, this momentum opens an accelerated path to challenge deeper overhead technical resistance zones.

Market Pulse & Strategy
Institutional commodity desks are executing an aggressive post-holiday asset reallocation strategy this afternoon. Automated trading networks have officially suspended their systematic short-grain expressions, rapidly pivoting capital back into tangible, climate-vulnerable hard assets to absorb the newly introduced mid-summer risk premium. In sharp contrast, industrial and energy-heavy derivatives are being consistently faded by multi-asset funds, as the reality of a fully normalized global maritime transit pipeline keeps a heavy lid on global fuel benchmarks.

Pro Takeaway: The weather market has officially arrived. The fact that soybean futures can capture over 50 cents of gains in a single post-holiday session proves that private models left themselves heavily exposed to an unyielding Midwest climate shift. Maintain maximum protection on remaining livestock inventory layers while letting your long grain lines capture the full extension of this environmental short-covering squeeze.

The grain bears ran straight into an absolute wall of fire over the holiday weekend. Seeing July Corn surge over fifteen cents to hit $4.75 and beans jump fifty cents proves exactly what independent operations have argued for weeks—this crop cannot handle an early-summer heat block without dropping condition points. 

Over in the cattle complex, the screen is playing a textbook post-holiday waiting game at $239 for live cattle. Packers are checking the retail counter by clipping Choice cuts down to $386.48, but with country cash holding an incredibly solid weighted average at $255.12, they simply don't have the supply leverage needed to push the countryside around this week.

Notes

The Regional Moisture Cliff: The quiet macro story of the week is the severe state-level divergence highlighted by the sudden heat dome. Production lines across the western fringes are absorbing the brunt of the atmospheric dryness, forcing regional basis values to step up aggressively to protect local commercial processing needs. For diversified operators managing feed yards in Nebraska, this localized crop squeeze is building an explicit, rigid floor under near-term ration breakevens just as country cash cattle settle into their summer baseline ranges.

The Rest of the Week

Wednesday, July 8: Regular weekly EIA crude oil and ethanol inventory data returns to its normal mid-week cycle. Watch closely to see if domestic biofuel blending lines expand on the back of rising grain costs.

Thursday, July 9: Weekly USDA Export Sales data. This will provide a critical look at whether international destination markets continue to chase domestic agricultural options despite the sudden post-holiday price surge.

Trader Strategy: Step up your trailing protective stops tightly behind your long grain allocations to insulate today's massive post-holiday green margins. In livestock, ignore the narrow near-term fluctuations in wholesale cutout numbers and confidently maintain your country cash show-list limits, utilizing the underlying structural calf scarcity to anchor your baseline margins through the summer transition.

#CornFutures #SoybeanSurge #WeatherMarket #BoxedBeef #CashCattle

WEEKLY AG MONEY FLOW & BASIS REPORT

Hedge Fund Positioning vs. Cash Market Reality
Date: Tuesday, June 30, 2026

THE BIG PICTURE: CRUDE STABILIZES NEAR $70 AS THE HEAT MOVES IN

The global energy complex has finally found its footing, shifting the broader commodity sectors from an active free-fall into a phase of volatile consolidation. Following a sweeping 23% plunge from its multi-month crisis peaks, front-month WTI Crude (/CL) spent the early session bouncing off major technical baselines near $70.77 per barrel. Speculative capital has completely finished pricing in the immediate layout of the Strait of Hormuz ceasefire, prompting a full asset rotation.

The focus of the agricultural sector has detached entirely from macro energy panic and locked directly onto localized climate fundamentals. With weather forecasting models validating a substantial heat dome expanding across the primary production belts over the next ten days, row-crop contracts are taking back ownership of their own balance sheets, inserting immediate seasonal premium structure back into the forward board curves.

MONEY FLOW BREAKDOWN

Oilseeds: +$14k (Weekly Flow) | Sentiment: Defensively Bullish

Key Driver: Funds actively covered defensive short blocks over the weekend. While raw prices are currently testing severe multi-month compression points near breakeven baselines, institutional accumulation returned on verified drops in crop condition ratings.

Grains: +$26k (Weekly Flow) | Sentiment: Bullish

Key Driver: Speculative capital is flowing back into early grain length. Early pollination and reproductive timelines are running aggressively ahead of historical 5-year tempos, maximizing technical sensitivity to immediate thermal peaks.

Softs: -$4k (Weekly Flow) | Sentiment: Neutral
Key Driver: Capital allocation across the tropical and logistics-heavy desks has flattened completely as real-time maritime shipping insurance premiums plateau alongside the broader energy floor.

Meats: -$19k (Weekly Flow) | Sentiment: Bearish / Retrenching

Key Driver: The aggressive cost-side relief rally seen mid-month hit a sharp wall of consumer retail pushback. Despite lower over-the-road freight indexes, general product destination pipelines are logging immediate backlogs.

TOTAL FLOW: +$17k | WEATHER PREMIUM RE-ENGAGEMENT

Note: Managed money flow from Monday Open has officially snapped its multi-week net liquidation sequence, re-establishing direct production-risk positioning across Chicago desks.

THE BASIS CHECK: NEBRASKA & IOWA FOCUS
The separation between macro board consolidation and actual physical pipeline tightness has turned regional elevators into absolute friction zones.

Eastern Nebraska: Local old-crop corn basis has aggressively defended its territory, holding firm at 25¢ to 40¢ under July. Commercial facilities attempting to capture regional crop protection steps have been entirely blocked from softening terms due to strict grower dynamic limits.

Western Iowa: Physical cash bids showed localized resistance to previous futures decay. State average cash corn edged up slightly to $4.46, while western Iowa cash beans verified structural support at $11.10. Northwest Iowa terminal soy bids closed wide but protective at 60¢–80¢ under July.

The Takeaway: The "Bin Wall" has gone completely structural. Producers have entirely written off the post-ceasefire macro correction and are actively withholding remaining old-crop inventory, gambling that the impending July weather dome forces commercial shorts to heavily bleed regional basis fields.

NOTES:

1. Crop Conditions Regression Begins

Yesterday afternoon’s highly anticipated USDA Crop Progress update officially verified the first material cracks in crop quality. The national Corn rating slipped 1 point down to 67% Good to Excellent, with 9% of the national crop already entering the critical silking stage (pacing comfortably ahead of the 6% five-year average). National Soybean conditions similarly regressed 1 point to 65% Good to Excellent, with blooming advanced to 19% complete.

2. The Wheat Harvest Ground Floor

The winter wheat harvest continues to advance at a rapid clip, now officially logged at 48% complete nationwide. National quality benchmarks remain firmly anchored in structural crisis, left completely unchanged at a dismal 26% Good to Excellent. This systemic, permanent quality deficit across early production channels continues to defend a rigid, non-negotiable floor beneath domestic livestock feed substitutions.

3. Biofuel Infrastructure Stabilization
The latest weekly EIA inventory tracking update verified that domestic fuel ethanol production registered a slight recovery, stabilizing at 1.09 million barrels per day. Total aggregate domestic ending stocks recorded a modest expansion, tracking upward to 24.585 million barrels. While processing margins are no longer displaying the extreme premiums of early spring, steady mandatory baseline blending margins are insulating regional commercial buyers.

4. Weather Overrides Macro Shadows

With corn and soy contracts now trading at deep multi-year values well below estimated producer breakeven targets, downward technical momentum has completely stalled out. Market participants are uniformly acknowledging that absent a catastrophic crop failure, production levels are fully calculated, passing the entire directional steering wheel to mid-summer moisture profiles.

SUMMARY
The macro energy liquidations have completely run their course, and the floor near $69.30 on WTI has held its ground. The marketplace is entering the true, high-stakes July weather gauntlet. With row-crop conditions officially beginning to tilt backward and early reproductive timelines moving at a blistering speed right into high-heat forecasts, any sustained failure of regional rainfall will spark an immediate, vertical short-covering cycle.

 #Corn #Soybeans #Trading #Commodities #NebraskaAg #IowaAg #CrudeStabilization #WeatherMarket #CropProgress #WheatCrisis

U.S. SEES OIL FLOWS RECOVERING FASTER 

The U.S. Energy Information Administration now expects global oil production and flows to return to near pre-war levels by late 2026, earlier than previously forecast. Most shut-in output is expected back by Q1 2027. The EIA also lowered its 2027 Brent crude forecast to $65 a barrel, citing improving supply.

text:
🌍 Nordic markets closed mostly lower on Tuesday as investors turned more cautious amid geopolitical uncertainty, higher oil prices, and broad weakness in technology and semiconductor stocks.

The OMX Nordic 40 Index fell 0.7%, with most of the selling concentrated in semiconductor and technology companies.

📉 Biggest losers

🔻 $SIVE (Sivers Semiconductors) plunged 11.7%, leading losses across the region.

🔻 Silex Microsystems dropped 9.0% as investors locked in profits after its strong rally in recent months.

🔻 Nordic Semiconductor fell 6.7%, tracking the broader global semiconductor selloff.

📈 Biggest gainers

✅ Kinnevik surged 11.3%.

✅ Solstad Maritime climbed more than 10% in Sweden and nearly 8% in Norway, supported by stronger energy and offshore sentiment.

Regional performance

• 🇩🇰 Denmark (OMX Copenhagen 20) +1.1%

• 🇫🇮 Finland (OMX Helsinki 25) -1.0%

• 🇸🇪 Sweden (OMX Stockholm 30) -0.9%

• 🇳🇴 Norway (Oslo Benchmark) +0.4%

• 🇮🇸 Iceland -1.0%

Across Europe, the STOXX Europe 600 slipped 0.6%, while the FTSE 100 managed a modest 0.1% gain.

Why did markets weaken?

The main driver was a shift toward risk-off sentiment after renewed tensions in the Strait of Hormuz pushed Brent crude oil up nearly 3%. Rising oil prices increase concerns that inflation could stay higher for longer, potentially delaying interest-rate cuts and putting pressure on high-growth sectors such as technology.

At the same time, investors continued taking profits in semiconductor stocks after a strong run this year, weighing on companies like $SIVE, Nordic Semiconductor, and Silex Microsystems.

Other markets

🛢️ Brent crude: +2.9%

₿ Bitcoin: +0.3% to around $63,880

🟢 U.S. Dollar Index: +0.1%

Long-term investor perspective

One weak trading session doesn’t change the bigger picture.

The long-term drivers for many Nordic companies—including AI infrastructure, semiconductors, industrial automation, renewable energy, and electrification—remain intact. However, when geopolitical tensions rise and oil prices jump, investors often reduce exposure to higher-growth stocks first, leading to short-term volatility.

Think of it like a strong company taking one step back after several steps forward. Markets rarely move in a straight line. Profit-taking and macro uncertainty can create temporary pullbacks even when the long-term business outlook remains unchanged.

Bottom line: Tuesday’s decline appears to be driven primarily by macro uncertainty, rising energy prices, and profit-taking, rather than a deterioration in company fundamentals. For long-term investors, the focus remains on earnings growth, AI investment, semiconductor demand, and future interest-rate expectations.

Iran MoU Implementation Talks Advance Amid Sanctions Relief

The past week centered on efforts to implement the June 17 US-Iran memorandum of understanding, including planned Doha discussions on frozen asset releases and oil sanctions waivers. Iranian officials prioritized economic relief provisions while managing domestic clerical support. Temporary sanctions relief on oil exports continued, with potential implications for trade flows through the Strait of Hormuz. Broader economic pressures including high inflation and capital outflows persisted as background context.

🛢️ ANOTHER TANKER STRUCK IN STRAIT OF HORMUZ AS MARITIME TENSIONS ESCALATE

The UK Maritime Trade Operations agency reports another tanker was hit by an unidentified projectile while passing through the Strait of Hormuz.

The vessel reportedly suffered structural damage, but no casualties or environmental impact were reported.

This follows two tanker strikes overnight — including a Qatari oil tanker. Doha has blamed Iran for the attack.

QATAR: IRAN BEARS “FULL LEGAL RESPONSIBILITY” FOR ATTACK ON TANKER NEAR STRAIT OF HORMUZ

Qatar condemned the attack on its tanker Al Rekayyat near the Strait of Hormuz, calling it an “unacceptable attack” on international navigation and global energy security.

Doha urged Tehran to stop actions threatening regional stability, stating Iran bears “full legal responsibility” for the attack and any consequences.

text:
Watch next EIA crude inventory data for flow confirmation; how might sustained low prices alter OPEC+ cohesion?

Research/education — not advice.

Welcome to this week’s Fortune Gulf Brief. We’ll be covering:  

  • Gulf oil flows return as market eyes potential glut  
  • Mubadala unlocks $25 billion credit portfolio for outside investors 
  • U.S.’ Lux Capital leads Gulf’s $30 million AI funding round 
  • Saudi courts China amid strained U.S. relations 

OPEC+ has agreed to raise oil production by a further 188,000 barrels per day from August, marking the fifth consecutive monthly increase in output quotas as the group continues to unwind its earlier production cuts. 

That brings the total increase in output quotas to around 940,000 barrels a day since the war began. 

The move comes as oil prices continue to ease amid Gulf states ramping up production and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz calming fears of major supply disruptions.  

Brent crude is now trading around $72 per barrel, down from its April peak of $126 per barrel and close to pre-conflict levels. 

Saudi Arabia, the world’s top exporter, shipped an average of 6.3 million barrels a day last week, restoring flows to almost 90% of February’s pre-war levels. 

Meanwhile, UAE oil exports have now overtaken pre-war levels, according to data compiled by energy intelligence company Kpler. 

The country, which formally exited OPEC+ on May 1, shipped 3.94 million barrels a day of crude and condensate in June.  

In addition to ramping up its production since leaving OPEC+, Kpler senior oil analyst Johannes Raubal said the UAE has also been drawing down crude inventories, further enhancing export volumes. 

But the surge in supply is beginning to raise concerns. Analysts at Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs warned last week that the market could be heading for a glut next year if producers continue pumping without consideration of demand. 

China, the world’s largest oil importer, remains one of the biggest question marks.  

The Middle East typically accounts for around half of China’s crude oil imports, but shipments declined in April to their lowest level in almost a decade, according to Kpler data. 

Despite cutting imports by roughly 5 million barrels a day compared with pre-war levels, it has yet to significantly increase its buying. 

Meanwhile, more than 60 million barrels of oil that were effectively stranded when the war broke out have now been released onto the market, following the signing of the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding, Bloomberg reported last week. 

It noted that UAE oil is traveling as far afield as the U.S. and is even being offered to buyers in Hawaii. 

Melissa Hancock
melissa.hancock@fortune.com

@Juuji311 @JavierBlas The U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains well below historical levels, and commercial crude inventories are also below their five-year average. Total U.S. crude oil inventories are therefore still relatively low by historical standards rather than "topped off."

Chart of the Day: $CL (Crude Oil)

Why this asset today?

Crude oil has bounced from a major support zone and broken out of its lower timeframe range. While the higher timeframe trend remains bearish, the short-term setup favors a pullback to the upside.

Any news or event?

U.S. crude inventories fell by 3.8 million barrels last week, while global supply remains tight as Gulf exports are still below pre-conflict levels. This is supporting oil prices in the short term.

Technical snapshot

• Trend: Bullish (short term)
• Key level: $69
• Support: $66
• Resistance: $78

What to watch for

• Above $69 → Bullish continuation toward $78
• Below $69 → Likely chop or retest of $66

Current view

Bullish in the short term. The lower timeframe breakout and supportive supply data suggest a relief rally, even though the higher timeframe trend remains bearish.

You can trade this setup on Futures INR on Mudrex
 https://t.co/IDMbmjYHPh

For learning purposes. Not investment advice.

#Crudeoil #Oiltrade #TechnicalAnalysis #Tradeoftheday

The Speculator’s Glossary Series Topic 46: Fundamental Analysis

Fundamentals aren't textbook GDP growth statistics; they are the "ultimate hands" of real-world supply and demand.
Amateurs think reading an earnings report is analysis; elite speculators harness satellite data to track crude inventories and big data to monitor global port throughput. In the short term, price is driven by sentiment; in the long run, it is dragged back by the iron laws of supply and demand. Trading without fundamentals is driving a supercar at night with your eyes shut.
At Bifu, we integrate top-tier global macro data feeds and real-time financial intel. We strip away media sensationalism to expose the nucleus of asset pricing. Comprehend the underlying supply and demand, and you start running before the tidal wave hits.

One Account, Trade the World.

My trading board has zero scheduled economic data on it today. No central bank, no tier-one release, nothing due. That quietly changes how I read every move.

With no data on the way, whatever moves is positioning and flow, not new information. A break on a no-print day is far easier to fake than one driven by an actual release, so I don't trust it the same and I don't chase it.

$USDCAD is the clean example. It has run from its late-April base near 1.355 up to the June highs and now sits pinned just under 1.42, extended, with no real pullback yet. On a quiet day I have no interest in buying that into the highs. I want it back near its rising 20-day average around 1.41, or a genuine reaction to tomorrow's US crude inventory print, before it earns a trade.

Tomorrow the calendar reloads: an overnight RBNZ decision, that crude number, a 10-year auction. So today the whole job is smaller. Levels marked, powder dry, ready for when the data actually shows up.

🚨 🛢️ TANKER WAR HITS BOTH OIL ROUTES AS HORMUZ AND KERCH SEND CRUDE ABOVE $70

WTI traded near $70.28 and Brent near $74.05 after tanker strikes hit the Strait of Hormuz while Ukraine’s drone units struck Russian fuel tankers near Kerch. One chokepoint threatens Gulf flows; the other squeezes Crimea-linked fuel logistics.

This is oil’s new problem: supply is not only about barrels, it is about ships surviving the route. Insurance, delays and rerouting now become part of the crude price.

Oil, tanker rates and gold hold the stronger trade; airlines and import-heavy equities absorb the cost.
